title:
Your Job Is Not
a Function
subtitle: The Universal Approximation Theorem already describes which jobs AI can replace. It also describes, by omission, which ones it cannot.
author: Yen Wee Lim

slides.headline: A 1989 proof changed everything
slides.point: Cybenko proved a neural network can approximate any continuous function. If a task maps inputs to outputs through a learnable pattern, a network can learn it.

slides.headline: 57% of work hours are automatable
slides.point: But fewer than 4% of entire jobs are fully replaceable. The gap between 57% and 4% is purpose.

slides.headline: Three fields, same finding
slides.point: AI approximates functions. It does not approximate purpose. No theorem covers the question of which functions matter.

slides.headline: Task layer vs purpose layer
slides.point: In extraction, credit risk, hiring, and software engineering: the function layer got cheaper. The purpose layer got more valuable.

slides.headline: This is a sorting function
slides.point: If your job has purpose, AI is a 10x multiplier. If your entire job is a function, the theorem already has your number.

slides.headline: The real worry
slides.point: Entry-level workers learn judgment by doing tasks. If AI handles the task layer, what is the apprenticeship path for purpose?
